does: This paper develops a risk taxonomy and policy framework for personalizing large language models using individual user feedback while maintaining safety and alignment boundaries. The authors analyze how micro-level preference learning—adapting models to individual user values and communication norms—can improve alignment with diverse preferences, but create normative challenges around acceptable personalization limits. The paper systematizes the risks involved in deploying personalized LLMs, identifying tensions between respecting legitimate preference diversity and preventing harmful or unsafe model outputs. It moves beyond aggregate alignment approaches like RLHF by acknowledging that different users may legitimately disagree on values, language norms, and ideologies, requiring a framework that defines which forms of personalization are societally acceptable versus problematic.
connects: This work builds on established alignment research including reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and red-teaming approaches, which have formed the foundation for safety improvements in LLMs. However, it responds to a fundamental limitation of these aggregate methods: they treat user preferences as monolithic rather than plural. The paper extends personalization literature in machine learning by focusing specifically on the safety and normative dimensions that prior work on personalized NLP systems largely overlooked. It connects to broader AI ethics and governance discussions around value pluralism, user autonomy, and societal constraints on AI behavior—drawing on philosophy and policy perspectives beyond traditional ML safety. The work acknowledges that RLHF's single-aggregate-model approach cannot capture legitimate human value disagreement.
enables: This framework enables more principled deployment of personalized LLMs by clarifying which adaptations enhance user-model alignment responsibly versus those that risk enabling harmful or polarized outputs. It provides a foundation for future technical work on personalization methods that respect safety bounds—such as preference learning systems that allow customization within defined guardrails. The taxonomy and policy framework can guide product designers integrating LLMs into interfaces like ChatGPT and search engines on how to implement personalization responsibly. It opens space for research on technical mechanisms that enforce personalization boundaries and methods for detecting when user preferences cross acceptable thresholds. The work also points toward governance approaches balancing user autonomy with societal safety, potentially informing regulatory frameworks for AI systems as personalization becomes standard in deployed models.
